plainMeaning: The sacred essence visualized in this meditation, contemplated step by step through its four and then five colours, becomes the power that accomplishes everything the devotee seeks.
recitation: tattvam atra caturvarṇī, caturvarṇa-vimiśritā; pañca-varṇa-krama-dhyātā, sarva-kāryakarī bhavet.

wordByWord.gloss: the essence/reality (tattva), here — i.e. in this very meditation (atra)
wordByWord.etymology: Sandhi of tattvam (tattva = 'thatness, essence, ultimate reality', from tat 'that' + abstract suffix -tva) + atra ('here, in this place/practice').
wordByWord.notes: tattva is grammatically neuter, yet every adjective qualifying it in this verse is feminine (caturvarṇī, vimiśritā, krama-dhyātā, kāryakarī); the feminine concord points to the meditated essence taken as a personified vidyā/mantra-power. See notes.

wordByWord.etymology: catur ('four') + varṇa ('colour'; also 'letter, syllable') + feminine suffix -ī.
wordByWord.notes: varṇa is deliberately double-edged here: a four-coloured visual form and equally a four-syllabled bīja.

wordByWord.etymology: catur ('four') + varṇa; first member of the compound caturvarṇa-vimiśritā.
wordByWord.notes: Restates the fourfold colouring as the material that is then 'blended'.

wordByWord.gloss: blended, thoroughly intermixed (with the four varṇas)
wordByWord.notes: Describes the four colours/syllables as fused into one composite tattva, not held separate.

wordByWord.etymology: pañca ('five') + varṇa ('colour; syllable'); first member of pañca-varṇa-krama-dhyātā.
wordByWord.notes: The fourfold form is now meditated as fivefold — in Jain mantra-dhyāna the five varṇas standardly map to the five Parameṣṭhins.

wordByWord.etymology: krama ('step, sequence, order') + dhyāta (past participle of √dhyai 'to meditate, contemplate'), feminine -ā.
wordByWord.notes: krama is the operative word: the colours must be visualized in their fixed succession, not at random.

wordByWord.gloss: accomplisher of [all] tasks; bringer-about of every undertaking
wordByWord.etymology: kārya (gerundive of √kṛ, 'that which is to be done, task, aim') + karī (feminine of kara, 'doer, effecting'), giving sarva-kārya-karī, 'doing all that is to be done'.
wordByWord.notes: Feminine, agreeing with the personified tattva/vidyā.
The optative frames the result as a settled consequence of correct practice: 'it comes to be so.'

literalTranslation: The essence here is four-coloured, blended with the four colours; meditated in the sequence of five colours, it becomes the doer of all tasks.
idiomaticTranslation: This essence, contemplated here, is fourfold in its colours and has those four hues fused into one; when it is then meditated in the ordered succession of five colours, it becomes the power that accomplishes every aim.

elaboration.verseByVerseCommentary: This verse supplies the colour-coding for the visualization just set up: where the previous verse raised the tattva through the navel-channel to the brahma-randhra upon the eight-petalled lotus, this one tells the meditator how that tattva is to look and be held in mind. The whole turn rests on the double sense of varṇa, which means both 'colour' and 'syllable/letter'. So caturvarṇī reads simultaneously as 'four-coloured' and 'four-syllabled': the essence is at once a chromatic form and a four-lettered bīja. caturvarṇa-vimiśritā insists the four are blended (vimiśritā) — fused into a single composite radiance rather than held apart. The pivot is pañca-varṇa-krama-dhyātā: the same essence is then meditated as fivefold, and crucially in krama, the fixed sequence. The progression four → five is the point — the fourfold form is completed into the fivefold by adding the final varṇa (anticipating the kṣipa-oṃ-svāhā syllables and the pañcāṅga placement of the very next verse). The string of feminine endings (caturvarṇī, vimiśritā, krama-dhyātā, kāryakarī) over a neuter tattvam signals that the essence is being handled as a living vidyā — a mantra-power addressed almost as a goddess. The optative bhavet ('it becomes') states the yield calmly, as the natural fruit of correct, ordered practice rather than a wish: sarva-kāryakarī — she who effects every task. The verse therefore reads as the technical core of the meditation manual the preface flags: not praise, but the colour-and-sequence rubric by which the heart-borne mantra-king is to be visualized.
elaboration.doctrinalContext: The fivefold-colour scheme is not arbitrary: in Jain mantra-dhyāna the five varṇas conventionally correspond to the five Parameṣṭhins of the Namaskāra — Arihanta (white), Siddha (red), Ācārya (yellow/golden), Upādhyāya (blue/green) and Sādhu (black) — so meditating the tattva 'in the sequence of five colours' folds the whole field of veneration into a single visualized essence. Doctrinally the verse marries tāntric technique to the Jain understanding of dhyāna: concentrated, ordered contemplation (krama-dhyāna) is itself a cause of result, here phrased as sarva-kārya-karī. For the tradition this 'all tasks' spans both the worldly protection the hymn promises and, ultimately, the inner accomplishment (siddhi) the colophon names — the same dual register that runs through Pārśva's bhayahara stotras.
elaboration.practicalRelevance: In practice the reciter does not merely say the words but builds the picture: hold the heart-seated essence as a single blended four-coloured form, then 'open' it through the five colours one after another in their set order, syllable matched to hue. The discipline the verse demands is krama — sequence and steadiness — so the takeaway for a modern practitioner is that the protective fruit follows from ordered, unhurried visualization, not speed of repetition.

crossReferences.text: Pañca-Parameṣṭhi five-colour meditation of the Namaskāra (white/red/yellow/blue-green/black)
Preceding verse 5 (nābhi-nāla → brahma-randhra, aṣṭa-dala padma) — the body-map this colouring fills
Following verse 7 (kṣipa oṃ svāhā; pañcāṅga protective placement) — completes the fivefold scheme

sources.title: Varṇa — definitions (incl. Mantraśāstra: 'colour' and 'letter/syllable')
sources.consultedFor: Confirming the dual sense of varṇa (colour and syllable/letter) operative in caturvarṇī / pañca-varṇa, and its specific use in mantra-science.

sources.title: Tattva — definitions (essence/reality; principle meditated upon)
sources.consultedFor: Glossing tattva as the 'essence/reality' that is the object of this visualization.

sources.title: Ṇamōkāra (Navakāra) Mantra — Pañca-Parameṣṭhi
sources.consultedFor: The Jain five-fold scheme (five Parameṣṭhins) underlying the standard five-colour mantra meditation referenced in doctrinalContext.

sources.title: Pārśvanātha (23rd Tīrthaṅkara) and his śāsana-devatās
sources.consultedFor: Situating the meditated tattva within the Pārśva mantra-stotra tradition.

notes: Verse-specific caveats: (1) varṇa is intentionally bivalent ('colour' and 'syllable'); the preface's structureArc treats v.6 as the 'four- and five-coloured tattva', so colour is primary while the syllable sense is retained. (2) The adjectives caturvarṇī/vimiśritā/krama-dhyātā/kāryakarī are feminine though tattvam is neuter; this is read as the essence personified as a vidyā/mantra-power — an interpretation, not an explicit statement of the verse. (3) The verse names a four→five colour progression but does not itself enumerate which colours or their order; the Parameṣṭhin mapping (white/red/yellow/blue-green/black) is the conventional Jain scheme supplied as context, not a literal gloss from this line. (4) Reading follows the book's printed Sanskrit verbatim; danda/punctuation in the source is retained and not glossed as lexical tokens.
